explanation.title: What is HTML and What Does It Build?
explanation.content:
# What is HTML? Imagine building a house. Before you paint the walls or lay the carpets, you need the bricks and beams — the raw structure that holds everything up. **HTML** (HyperText Markup Language) is exactly that structural layer for every webpage you have ever visited. HTML is not a programming language — it does not perform calculations or make decisions. It is a **markup language**, which means it uses special tags to label pieces of content and tell the browser what role each piece plays: this is a heading, this is a paragraph, this is an image, this is a link. When your browser receives an HTML file, it reads the tags and renders each piece of content according to its role. ## A Brief History HTML was invented by Tim Berners-Lee in 1991 as a way to share documents between scientists over the internet. The current standard is **HTML5**, introduced in 2014, which added native support for video, audio, forms, and semantic elements. ## The Three Languages of the Web Every webpage is built from three languages working together: | Language | Role | Analogy | |---|---|---| | **HTML** | Structure and content | The bricks and walls | | **CSS** | Visual styling | The paint and decoration | | **JavaScript** | Behaviour and interaction | The electricity and plumbing | In this course you will learn HTML first, then CSS, because structure must exist before style can be applied. ## What HTML Files Look Like An HTML file is a plain text file saved with the `.html` extension. You can open it in any text editor to write it, and in any browser to view it. No installation is required — every device already has a browser. ## Tags, Elements, and Attributes HTML content is wrapped in **tags**. Most tags come in pairs: ```html

This is a paragraph.

``` - `

` is the **opening tag** - `

` is the **closing tag** (note the forward slash) - Together with the content, they form an **element** Tags can also carry **attributes** — extra information written inside the opening tag: ```html Visit CodeGuru ``` Here `href` is the attribute name and `"https://codeguru.ai"` is its value. In the code example below, you will see a minimal but complete HTML page using the most essential tags.

codeExample.title: Your First HTML Page
codeExample.code:
Hello, World!

This is my first HTML page.

HTML uses tags to give content meaning.

Visit CodeGuru AI
codeExample.explanation:
- `<!DOCTYPE html>` — tells the browser this is HTML5; always the very first line
- `<html lang="en">` — the root element; `lang` helps screen readers pronounce content
- `<head>` — contains metadata the browser reads but does not display
- `<meta charset="UTF-8">` — allows all characters including Indian scripts and emoji
- `<title>` — text shown in the browser tab and search results
- `<body>` — all visible page content goes here
- `<h1>` — the most important heading; every page should have exactly one
- `<a href="...">` — a hyperlink; `href` specifies the destination URL

exercise.title: Build Your Personal Introduction Page
exercise.instructions:
Create a complete HTML page that introduces you. The page must include: the full DOCTYPE and html/head/body structure, a title tag set to 'About Me', one h1 heading with your name, one paragraph describing what you study or do, and one anchor tag linking to any website. The page must be valid HTML5.

Expected: a complete page with DOCTYPE, head, body, h1, p, and a tags all present.
exercise.starterCode:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <title>About Me</title>
</head>
<body>

  <!-- Add your h1 heading here -->

  <!-- Add a paragraph about yourself here -->

  <!-- Add a link to any website here -->

</body>
</html>
exercise.solutionCode:
Rahul Sharma

I am a first-year computer science student at Delhi University.

Visit CodeGuru AI
hints: 💡 Place your h1 tag between the opening body tag and the closing body tag.

💡 A paragraph is written as:

Your text here.

— both tags are required.
💡 A link is written as: Link text — the href attribute holds the URL.

questions.question: What does HTML stand for?
options: Hyper Transfer Markup Language
HyperText Markup Language
High-level Text Management Language
Hyper Tool Marking Language
questions.explanation: HTML stands for HyperText Markup Language. HyperText refers to text that contains links to other documents. Markup means the language uses tags to annotate or label content, telling the browser what role each piece of content plays on the page.

questions.question: Which part of an HTML document is displayed to the user in the browser window?
options: Everything inside the head element
Only the title element
Everything inside the body element
Only elements with a display attribute
questions.explanation: The body element contains all the content that the browser renders and displays to the user: headings, paragraphs, images, links, and so on. The head element contains metadata like the page title, character encoding, and CSS links, which the browser reads but does not render as visible content.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the DOCTYPE declaration at the top of an HTML file?
options: It sets the background colour of the page
It tells the browser which version of HTML the document uses
It links the HTML file to a CSS stylesheet
It defines the page title shown in the browser tab
questions.explanation: The DOCTYPE declaration instructs the browser which version of HTML the document is written in. Writing <!DOCTYPE html> triggers HTML5 mode, which ensures modern, standards-based rendering. Without it, browsers enter 'quirks mode' and may display pages inconsistently across different browsers.

explanation.title: Inside an HTML Document: Head, Body, and Metadata
explanation.content: # HTML Document Structure Every valid HTML page follows the same skeleton — a predictable hierarchy of nested elements. Understanding this structure is essential because every mistake you will encounter in HTML usually traces back to a missing or misplaced element in this hierarchy. ## The Full Skeleton ```html Page Title ``` ## The head Element — Invisible Metadata The `` contains information for the browser and search engines, not for the user: | Tag | Purpose | |---|---| | `` | Allow all characters and scripts | | `` | Enable correct scaling on mobile devices | | `` | The text search engines show under your link | | `` | Text shown in the browser tab | | `<link rel="stylesheet" href="...">` | Connect an external CSS file | | `<script src="...">` | Connect an external JavaScript file | ## The viewport meta Tag This tag is critical for mobile users: ```html <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0"> ``` Without it, mobile browsers zoom out to show the desktop version of your page, making text tiny and unreadable. With it, the page scales correctly to the device width. ## HTML Comments Comments are notes in your HTML that the browser ignores completely: ```html <!-- This is a comment --> <!-- Comments help explain your code --> ``` ## Nesting Rules Elements must be properly nested — every opened tag must be closed before its parent is closed: ```html <!-- Correct nesting --> <p>This is <strong>bold</strong> text.</p> <!-- Incorrect — strong is not closed before p --> <p>This is <strong>bold text.</p></strong> ``` Browsers often forgive incorrect nesting, but it causes unpredictable display bugs. Always nest correctly. In the code example below, you will see a fully structured HTML document with all head metadata filled in correctly.

codeExample.title: Complete Page Structure
codeExample.code:
Welcome to CodeGuru AI

Learn Python, JavaScript, Java, and HTML/CSS with AI guidance.

Built for Indian learners at affordable prices.
codeExample.explanation:
- `<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">` — the most important tag for mobile; makes the browser use the real device width
- `<meta name="description" content="...">` — shown by Google under your page link; keep under 160 characters
- `<style>` inside `<head>` — embeds CSS directly in the HTML file; use external `.css` files for larger projects
- `<!-- comment -->` — the browser ignores everything between `<!--` and `-->`
- `<strong>` — marks text as important; browsers render it bold by default

exercise.title: Build a Product Landing Page Shell
exercise.instructions:
Create a complete HTML document for a product page. Include in the head: charset meta, viewport meta, a description meta with content 'Affordable coding courses', and a title of 'CodeGuru Courses'. In the body, add one h1, one paragraph, and one HTML comment explaining what the page is. The document must be valid HTML5.

Expected: complete structure with all required head tags and visible body content.
exercise.starterCode:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <!-- Add charset meta here -->

  <!-- Add viewport meta here -->

  <!-- Add description meta here -->

  <title>CodeGuru Courses</title>
</head>
<body>

  <!-- Add a comment describing this page -->

  <!-- Add an h1 heading here -->

  <!-- Add a paragraph here -->

</body>
</html>
exercise.solutionCode:
Our Courses

Learn to code with AI-powered guidance at Indian prices.
hints: 💡 The charset meta tag is: <meta charset="UTF-8"> — no closing tag needed for meta elements.
💡 The viewport meta tag is: <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
💡 A comment is written as: <!-- your comment text here --> — the browser ignores everything inside.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the viewport meta tag in HTML?
options: It sets the font size for all text on the page
It enables correct page scaling on mobile devices
It connects the HTML page to a CSS file
It sets the page background colour
questions.explanation: The viewport meta tag tells mobile browsers to scale the page to the device's actual screen width rather than simulating a full desktop screen. Without it, mobile browsers zoom out so the page fits on the small screen, making all text tiny and unreadable. It is essential for any page that should work on phones.

questions.question: Which HTML element contains information about the page that is not displayed to the user?
options: body
header
head
meta
questions.explanation: The head element is the container for all page metadata: the title shown in the browser tab, character encoding, viewport settings, CSS and JavaScript file links, and search engine descriptions. None of this content appears in the visible page. The body element contains all the visible content.

explanation.title: Structuring Text with Headings, Paragraphs, and Inline Tags
explanation.content:
# Headings and Paragraphs Text is the foundation of almost every webpage. HTML provides specific elements for every kind of text, from page titles down to individual important words. Choosing the right element is important for accessibility, search engine optimisation, and visual clarity. ## The Six Heading Levels HTML has six levels of headings, `

` through `

`: ```html

Main Page Title

Section Heading

Subsection

Sub-subsection

Minor heading
Smallest heading
``` Key rules: - Use only **one `

`** per page — it tells search engines and screen readers what the page is about - Headings must be used in **logical order** — never skip from h1 to h4 - Choose heading levels based on **document structure**, not visual size ## Paragraphs ```html

A paragraph groups related sentences into a block of text.

Each paragraph element creates vertical spacing above and below.

``` Browsers collapse all whitespace inside HTML to a single space. To force a line break within a paragraph, use `
`: ```html

Line one.
Line two on the same paragraph.

``` ## Inline Text Elements These elements style or label individual words or phrases inside a paragraph: | Element | Default appearance | Semantic meaning | |---|---|---| | `` | **Bold** | Importance | | `` | *Italic* | Emphasis | | `` | Underline | Stylistic | | `` | Highlighted | Highlighted | | `` | Smaller | Fine print | | `` | ~~Strikethrough~~ | Deleted text | | `` | Subscript | H₂O | | `` | Superscript | x² | | `` | `Monospace` | Code snippet | ## Horizontal Rule and Line Break ```html

``` Both `
` and `
` are **void elements** — they have no content and no closing tag. In the code example below, you will see all six heading levels and several inline text elements used in a realistic article layout.

codeExample.title: Article Text Elements
codeExample.code:
The Complete Python Guide

Last updated: 2025 | 10 min read

What is Python?

Python is a powerful yet beginner-friendly programming language used worldwide.

Why Learn Python?

Python powers artificial intelligence, web development, and data science.

Key Facts

Python was created in 1991. The latest version is Python 3. Water is H2O — just for demo!

A Simple Example

The classic first program uses the print() function:

Getting Started

Install Python, then run:
Open terminal and type python --version.

Summary

Python is difficult. Python is easy to learn!
codeExample.explanation:
- `

` through `

` — three heading levels establish the content hierarchy for search engines and screen readers - `powerful` — bold text that also signals importance to assistive technology - `beginner-friendly` — italic text that signals stress emphasis - `artificial intelligence` — highlights text with a yellow background by default - `
` — a horizontal line representing a thematic break; a void element with no closing tag - `print()` — renders text in a monospace font to indicate computer code

exercise.title: Write a Formatted Bio Article
exercise.instructions:
Create a bio page for a fictional person. Use: one h1 for the person's full name, one h2 for 'About', a paragraph with at least one strong and one em tag, one h2 for 'Skills', a paragraph with at least one code tag, one hr between sections, and one small tag for a footer note. The page must have complete HTML5 structure.

Expected: page with h1, two h2 elements, hr, and inline text elements all used correctly.
exercise.starterCode:
About

Skills
exercise.solutionCode:
Priya Verma

About

Priya is a full-stack developer with three years of experience.

Skills

She writes Python and JavaScript every day.

Last updated: January 2025
hints:
💡 Place the h1 directly after the opening body tag with the person's name:

Full Name

💡 Inline tags go inside a p tag:

She is a developer with experience.
💡 The hr tag is a void element — write it as <hr> with no closing tag and no content.

questions.question: How many h1 elements should a well-structured HTML page have?
options: As many as needed — one per section
Exactly one — it represents the main topic of the page
Between two and five
None — h1 is deprecated in HTML5
questions.explanation: A well-structured HTML page should have exactly one h1 element representing the page's main topic. Search engines use the h1 to understand what the page is about, and screen readers use it as a navigation landmark. Using multiple h1 elements dilutes the page's focus for both users and search engines.

questions.question: What is the semantic difference between strong and b in HTML?
options: There is no difference — they render identically
strong signals importance to browsers and assistive technology; b is purely visual bold
b is stronger emphasis than strong
strong only works inside headings; b works everywhere
questions.explanation: Both strong and b render text in bold by default, but they carry different semantic meanings. strong signals that the text is of serious importance — screen readers may announce it with extra emphasis. b is purely presentational bold with no extra meaning. HTML5 recommends using strong for important text and b only when no semantic meaning is intended.

questions.question: Which element creates a line break without starting a new paragraph?
options: <lb>
<nl>
<line>
questions.explanation:
The br element (break) inserts a single line break within the current paragraph or block without creating a new paragraph element. It is a void element — written as
with no closing tag. Use it sparingly; for separating unrelated content, a new p element is more semantically appropriate.
